children.value: NISAR GCOV products are delivered in HDF5, or Hierarchical Data Format version 5. HDF5 is a file format designed to efficiently store, organize, and access large scientific datasets. GCOV uses HDF5 to systematically organize radar data and metadata in a way that is both efficient and easy to read, share, and analyze.

HDF was originally developed by the National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA) at the University of Illinois to support data sharing within the scientific community. HDF5 represents a significant redesign compared to earlier versions of HDF, with a more flexible and powerful internal structure. For additional details, users can consult the official HDF documentation at
At a high level, an HDF5 file functions as a container that organizes data into a hierarchy of objects, such as

children.title: A folder within an HDF5 file

children.title: A folder within an HDF5 file

children.value: is a folder within an HDF5 file. Groups can hold datasets, datatypes and other groups (subfolders). In essence, groups act like directories on computers. Path navigation is identical to that or Unix path navigation:

children.title: A collection of data values stored in an HDF5 file

children.value: is where the actual data lives. This might be an array or table stored within the HDF5 file. Each dataset will include the data, a

children.title: Describing the shape of the data
What values are: integers, floats, etc

children.value: , and other optional attributes such as units, range, time, and other descriptions.

children.value: rather than storing the data itself. Attributes provide important context that helps correctly interpret values within a dataset. Common examples include:
Descriptions of what the data represent

Processing details.
Storing this information with the data helps ensure that datasets can be understood and used correctly without relying on external documentation.
An HDF5 datatype describes the kind of data that is being stored. A datatype explains how to interpret a dataset and how it is stored. Datatypes are categorized into three types:

children.title: Basic building blocks
Combinations of atomic types
Ways of storing and sharing datatypes
Basic building blocks

children.value: are typically the simplest datatypes. They serve as building blocks for more complex datatypes. Common atomic datatypes include:

children.title: Customized versions of atomic datatypes

children.value: in most cases, are custom versions of Atomic types. Derived datatypes are ideal for custom N-bit integers and floating-point numbers. They are useful for storing nonstandard data in optimal ways.
Derived datatypes are useful becasue they:
Allow ustom storage with specific numbers of bits
Save values that don’t follow standard number formats
Match the way the data was originally recorded
The HDF5 atomic datatypes used in NISAR GCOV products are summarized in Table 3-1.

children.title: Combinations of atomic types

children.value: combine multiple atomic datatypes in a more complex manner. Common types of composite datatypes include:

children.title: Fixed-size, multi-dimensional arrays treated as a single unit
One-dimensional arrays whose length can vary between elements
Collections of named fields, each with its own datatype
Map integer values to named labels
Ways of storing and sharing datatypes

children.value: are stored as objects within an HDF5 file. These are ideal for reusability and sharing.

Any datatype (atomic, derived, or composite) can be named.

Several kinds of metadata exist within a GCOV HDF5 file. Metadata provids contextual information that deescribes the data and how it was produced, which helps correctly interpret data. In GCOV products, both

children.title: Metadata that describes the entire file
Metadata that describes individual datasets
Metadata that describes the entire file

children.value: in GCOV products are stored as global attributes that describe the product as a whole. These attributes define the data conventions, product title, mission name, producing institution, reference documentation, and contact information.
group and are described in later sections.

children.title: Metadata specific to one acquisition

children.value: describe individual datasets within a GCOV HDF5 file. This metadata provides the information needed to understand what each dataset represents, how its values should be interpreted, and what values are considered valid. In GCOV products, variable metadata are stored as HDF5 attributes and are attached directly to each dataset.
Each GCOV HDF5 dataset includes a set of

children.title: Metadata that describes a group or dataset

children.value: that describe the data within a dataset. These attributes provide descriptive and contextual information, such as how missing values are represented, what the data measure, and the units of the data values. Common variable attributes in an HDF5 file include:
, describing the value used to represent missing or undefined data
, providing additional information about the dataset or how it was generated
, giving a human-readable, descriptive name for the dataset
, providing a standardized, case-sensitive name used to identify the dataset consistently
, describing the units of the dataset’s data values
, indicating the maximum theoretical valid value of the dataset
, indicating the minimum theoretical valid value of the dataset
Some datasets also include statistical attributes that summarize the data values. For real-valued datasets, these may include:
, the minimum value in the dataset
, the mean value of the dataset
, the maximum value of the dataset
, the sample standard deviation of the dataset
For complex-valued datasets, similar statistics may be provided separately for the real and imaginary components. Common attributes include:
, the minimum value of the real component
, the mean value of the real component
, the maximum value of the real component
, the sample standard deviation of the real component
, the minimum value of the imaginary component
, the mean value of the imaginary component
, the maximum value of the imaginary component
, the sample standard deviation of the imaginary component

Not all GCOV datasets include statistical attributes. The presence of these attributes depends on the dataset type. The datasets listed below are examples of GCOV datasets that may be populated with statistical attributes, grouped by whether they contain real-valued or complex-valued data. Availability of various frequencies will depend upon NISAR acquisition mode at the time of collection.
GCOV datasets represent radar-derived measurements organized by

children.title: A specific range of radar frequencies
The orientation of the radar signal

children.value: . The datasets available in a GCOV file depend on the LSAR acquisition mode during the time of acquisition.
Data acquired from different portions of the radar bandwidth are stored in separate frequency groups. Datasets collected from the lower portion of the spectrum are stored in the

children.title: Lower portion of the radar bandwidth

children.value: group, while datasets from the higher portion of the spectrum are stored in the

children.title: Higher portion of the radar signal

children.value: group. A GCOV product may contain data from only frequencA, or both frequencyA and frequencyB.

Within each frequency group, LSAR datasets are further organized by polarization combinations, such as HH, HV, VH, and VV. As a result, the exact set of datasets included in a GCOV file can vary between products, indicative of  acquisition configuration rather than missing data.

children.title: Linked to real locations on Earth

children.value: HDF5 datasets, meaning each dataset is associated with a real-world location on Earth. The georeferencing information follows

children.title: Standards for describing geospatial data

children.value: conventions, which provide standardized rules for describing spatial coordinates and map projections in Earth science data products.

children.title: Standards for describing geospatial data

children.value: , each georeferenced dataset must reference a

children.title: Information defining the map projection

children.value: dataset that informs the coordinate system used to assign the data to specific places on the Earth. In NISAR Level-2 products, this grid mapping is provided by a dataset named
, which is stored in the same group as the georeferenced dataset it describes.
Each georeferenced dataset includes an attribute called
, whose value is always set to “projection”. This attribute links the dataset to the corresponding projection dataset that contains the coordinate reference information.

Spatial coordinates for georeferenced datasets are provided using HDF5 dimension scales, which associate coordinate values with each data dimension. GCOV products use one-dimensional coordinate datasets for the horizontal X and Y directions, and for elevation where applicable. These coordinates represent the center location of each pixel in the georeferenced grid.
While most GCOV datasets are two-dimensional, some will be three-dimensional. 3D datasets are stored in the
group and provide geometric metadata regarding radar geometry instead of measured values.
, describing the mathematical shape used to model the Earth
, identifying the map projection using a numeric EPSG code
, describing the value added to all X coordinates to avoid negative values
, describing the value added to all Y coordinates to avoid negative values
, specifying the name of the grid mapping definition
, describing the flattening of the Earth’s ellipsoid
, defining the latitude used as the projection origin
, defining the longitude used as the projection origin
, describing the Earth’s equatorial radius used by the projection
, providing a text description of the spatial reference system
, identifying the UTM zone used for the projection
The following georeferenced datasets may be present in a GCOV product.
, describing the distance from the radar to the target
, describing the time when the radar is closest to the target
, describing the angle between the radar beam and the ground
, describing the X component of the radar line-of-sight direction
, describing the Y component of the radar line-of-sight direction
, describing the X component of the satellite motion direction
, describing the Y component of the satellite motion direction
, describing the elevation angle of the radar beam
, describing the satellite velocity along its ground track

children.title: A single data file covering a specific area on Earth

children.value: represents a single, spatially defined data product that follows the

children.title: A system for dividing the Earth into fixed data tiles

children.value: designed for the NISAR mission. A single granule will cover a ground area of approximately 240 km × 240 km. Exact

children.title: The area on Earth covered by the data

children.value: may vary depending on frame location.

All GCOV granules are provided on a fixed, geocoded output grid, meaning the data are mapped to consistent geographic coordinates rather than the original radar acquisition geometry. This ensures that GCOV products are spatially aligned and easy to compare across regions and acquisition times.

Each field in the filename represents a specific property of the GCOV product. Below is the general file naming structure for GCOV data:

— Bandwidth mode of the primary and secondary bands
